title: Intel 8257 DMA Controller
examTip: 8257 is a 4-channel DMA controller. Each channel has a 16-bit Address Register and a 16-bit Terminal Count (TC) Register (14 bits for count, 2 bits for read/write/verify code). Priority Resolver executes Fixed (CH0 > CH1 > CH2 > CH3) or Rotating priority. Signals: HRQ (Hold Request to CPU), HLDA (Hold Acknowledge from CPU), AEN (Address Enable).
theory:
The Intel 8257 is a Programmable Direct Memory Access (DMA) controller. It is a 40-pin DIP chip designed to transfer data between external I/O peripherals and memory at high speeds with minimal CPU intervention, which greatly increases system performance by taking over the system bus during transfers.

Key Components:
1. DMA Channels (CH0 – CH3): Four independent channels. Each channel has: 
   - DMA Address Register (16-bit): holds the memory address to access. It automatically increments or decrements after each transfer.
   - Terminal Count (TC) Register (16-bit): bits 0-13 specify the byte count to transfer. Bits 14-15 specify the DMA action (Verify, Write, or Read).
2. Priority Resolver: Determines which channel gets bus priority if multiple DREQ signals arrive at once. Modes are Fixed Priority (CH0 > CH1 > CH2 > CH3) and Rotating Priority (highest priority rotates to the channel that just finished).
3. Mode Set & Status Registers: Configuration registers. Enables/disables individual channels, sets priority modes, and monitors when a channel hits its Terminal Count (TC).
4. Read/Write and Control Logic: In Slave Mode (when CPU controls system bus), it reads status or writes parameters to the 8257. In Master Mode (when 8257 controls system bus), it generates MEMR, MEMW, IOR, IOW control signals.
5. Control signals:
   - HRQ (Hold Request): sent to CPU to request control of system bus.
   - HLDA (Hold Acknowledge): active-high response from CPU releasing the bus.
   - AEN (Address Enable): disables external system buffers to isolate the CPU from the bus.
   - ADSTB (Address Strobe): latches the upper address bits (A8-A15) from the data bus.
keyPoints: Four independent DMA channels (CH0 – CH3), each with starting Address and TC registers
Priority Resolver selects channels using Fixed (CH0–CH3) or Rotating priority rules
Generates HRQ (Hold Request) to host CPU; receives HLDA (Hold Acknowledge) to gain bus control
Isolates the CPU from system buses during DMA operation using the AEN (Address Enable) line
Terminal Count (TC) signal indicates completion of all programmed byte transfers for a channel
useCases: High-speed disk controllers (e.g. floppy drives) transferring sectors directly to RAM
CRT terminal controllers and screen refresh buffers retrieving characters from system memory
High-speed communication ports transferring large packets without overloading the CPU
